treatments.name: Lorlatinib (Lorbrena) — First-Line Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 CROWN trial (N=296) is the definitive RCT. At 60-month median follow-up, median PFS not reached with lorlatinib vs 9.3 months with crizotinib (HR 0.19). This is the longest PFS ever reported for any single-agent targeted therapy across all metastatic solid tumors.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year PFS rate 60% vs 8% for crizotinib. 5-year OS probability 76% (phase 2 data). 92% reduction in intracranial progression. Median PFS not reached at 5 years — unprecedented in metastatic oncology.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (2021), EMA approved. NCCN category 1 preferred first-line option for ALK+ NSCLC. Widely available at all major oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant metabolic toxicity: hypercholesterolemia (82%), hypertriglyceridemia (61%), weight gain (81% of patients, median 4.5 kg). CNS effects include cognitive changes (28%), mood changes (21%). Requires lipid-lowering therapy in 81% of patients. Manageable but requires close monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets ALK fusion — perfect biomarker match for this ALK+ patient. PD-L1 status irrelevant for ALK TKI efficacy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Third-generation ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitor (macrocyclic design) that potently inhibits ALK and ROS1 kinases. Designed to overcome resistance mutations including G1202R solvent front mutation. Excellent CNS penetration due to ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, providing intracranial activity against brain metastases.

treatments.name: Alectinib (Alecensa) — First-Line Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 ALEX trial (N=303) with final OS data. First global randomized study to show clinically meaningful OS improvement for a next-gen ALK TKI vs crizotinib. Median PFS 34.8 vs 10.9 months (HR 0.43).

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 81.1 months vs 54.2 months with crizotinib. 5-year OS rate 62.5% vs 45.5%. Excellent CNS control with intracranial response rate 64% vs 21% crizotinib. Mature OS data is a major advantage over lorlatinib where OS remains immature.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (2017), EMA approved. NCCN category 1 preferred first-line option. Longest real-world experience among next-gen ALK TKIs. Widely available globally.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than lorlatinib overall. Main toxicities include myalgia, constipation, edema, hepatotoxicity (AST/ALT elevation). Weight gain in 18% (10%+ increase). Less CNS toxicity and less metabolic disruption than lorlatinib.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets ALK fusion kinase. Perfect biomarker match for ALK+ disease. PD-L1 status irrelevant.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Second-generation selective ALK inhibitor with high CNS penetration. Not a P-glycoprotein substrate, allowing effective blood-brain barrier crossing. Inhibits ALK with high selectivity, resulting in fewer off-target effects compared to crizotinib.

treatments.name: Brigatinib (Alunbrig) — First-Line Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 ALTA-1L trial (N=275) demonstrated superior PFS vs crizotinib. Median PFS 30.8 months vs 9.2 months (HR 0.48). 4-year PFS rate 36%. Final analysis published.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 30.8 months with 4-year PFS rate of 36%. Intracranial ORR of 78% in patients with measurable brain metastases. OS data did not show significant difference (HR 0.81) likely due to crossover.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (2020 for first-line), EMA approved. NCCN category 1 preferred first-line option. Widely available.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated. Key toxicities include CPK elevation, lipase elevation, hypertension, and early-onset pneumonitis (ILD) in ~3-5% of patients. Dose escalation from 90mg to 180mg after 7 days mitigates early pulmonary events.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets ALK fusion kinase. Perfect biomarker match for ALK+ disease. PD-L1 status irrelevant.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Second-generation ALK inhibitor with broad spectrum activity against ALK resistance mutations. Demonstrates potent inhibition of ALK including G1202R mutation. Good CNS penetration with demonstrated intracranial activity.

treatments.name: Ensartinib (Xalkori) — First-Line Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 eXalt3 trial (N=290) demonstrated superior PFS vs crizotinib. Median PFS 25.8 months vs 12.7 months (HR 0.56, p=0.0007). FDA approved 2024.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 25.8 months vs 12.7 for crizotinib. Confirmed ORR 74% vs 67%. Intracranial response 64% vs 21% in patients with brain metastases. OS did not significantly differ (HR 0.88, p=0.46).

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (2024) for first-line ALK+ metastatic NSCLC. NCCN preferred option. Newest approved ALK TKI with growing availability.

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable safety profile. Main toxicities include rash (56%), nausea, pruritis, AST/ALT elevation. Less metabolic and CNS toxicity compared to lorlatinib.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets ALK fusion kinase. Perfect biomarker match for ALK+ disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Second-generation ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitor with activity against multiple ALK resistance mutations including L1196M and C1156Y. Also inhibits MET, ABL, AXL, EPHA2, LTK, ROS1, and SLK. Good CNS penetration.

treatments.name: Crizotinib (Xalkori) — First-Generation ALK TKI

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 PROFILE 1014 and PROFILE 1007 trials established crizotinib efficacy. First ALK TKI approved. Extensive Phase 3 RCT data but superseded by next-gen agents.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 10.9 months in first-line (PROFILE 1014). Substantially inferior to alectinib (34.8 mo), brigatinib (30.8 mo), and lorlatinib (NR at 60 mo). No OS advantage shown. Limited CNS penetration.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (2011), widely available globally. Low cost as generic versions available in many markets. No longer preferred first-line per NCCN (useful in other circumstances).

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety. Vision disorders (62%), diarrhea (61%), nausea (55%), vomiting (46%), edema. Generally manageable. Less metabolic toxicity than lorlatinib.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets ALK fusion. Perfect biomarker match but inferior efficacy compared to newer ALK TKIs for this indication.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: First-generation ALK/ROS1/MET tyrosine kinase inhibitor. Competitively inhibits ALK kinase activity. Limited CNS penetration as it is a P-glycoprotein substrate, leading to inferior intracranial disease control compared to next-gen agents.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — NCCN Other Recommended (not preferred)

treatments.name: Lorlatinib After 2nd-Gen ALK TKI Failure (Sequential Strategy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 pivotal study with multiple expansion cohorts. In post-2nd-gen TKI patients, ORR 35-47%. Retrospective and real-world data support 35-45% response rate when transitioning from alectinib to lorlatinib.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR of 35-47% in post-alectinib/ceritinib setting. Responses driven by on-target ALK mutations (especially G1202R). Median PFS approximately 5-7 months. Critical salvage option that extends disease control.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for ALK+ NSCLC after progression on one or more ALK TKIs. Widely available. Standard approach when used sequentially.

safety_profile.rationale: Same metabolic and CNS toxicity profile as first-line use. Hyperlipidemia, weight gain, cognitive effects. May be more challenging in patients already treated and potentially debilitated.

biomarker_match.rationale: Most effective when on-target ALK resistance mutations (especially G1202R) are present. Liquid biopsy or tissue NGS to identify resistance mutations guides optimal use.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Third-generation ALK TKI used sequentially after failure of 2nd-gen ALK TKIs. Designed to overcome G1202R solvent front mutation and other resistance mutations that emerge during prior ALK TKI therapy. Brain-penetrant.

key_evidence.journal: Journal of Thoracic Oncology / Lancet Oncology

biomarker_requirements: Prior ALK TKI exposure with progression
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — for post-ALK TKI progression

treatments.name: Ceritinib (Zykadia) — First-Line Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 ASCEND-4 trial (N=376) demonstrated superior PFS vs platinum-pemetrexed chemotherapy. Median PFS 16.6 months vs 8.1 months (HR 0.55). Established first-line efficacy.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 16.6 months vs 8.1 months for chemo. ORR 73% vs 27%. Shorter PFS than alectinib, brigatinib, or lorlatinib. Generally considered inferior first-line option compared to newer ALK TKIs but retains role in sequencing.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (2017 for first-line). NCCN listed but not preferred (other recommended) due to inferior efficacy and tolerability vs alectinib/lorlatinib. Widely available.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant GI toxicity: diarrhea (85%), nausea (69%), vomiting (66%). Hepatotoxicity with ALT elevation. Dose reduced from 750mg to 450mg with food to improve tolerability. Worst tolerability among next-gen ALK TKIs.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets ALK fusion kinase. Perfect biomarker match for ALK+ disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Second-generation ALK inhibitor that inhibits ALK, IGF-1R, InsR, and ROS1. Potent against wild-type ALK and several crizotinib-resistant mutations. Moderate CNS penetration.

treatments.name: Neladalkib (NVL-655) — 4th-Generation ALK TKI

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 ALKOVE-1 trial with 253 evaluable TKI-pretreated patients. ORR 31% overall, 46% in lorlatinib-naive, 86% in TKI-naive exploratory cohort. FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation. Phase 3 ALKAZAR trial initiated 2025.

survival_benefit.rationale: In lorlatinib-naive patients, ORR 46% with 80% of responses lasting 12+ months. In TKI-naive patients, preliminary ORR 86% with CR rate 9%. Designed to overcome compound mutations (double mutations) that cause lorlatinib resistance.

accessibility.rationale: Available only via clinical trials (ALKOVE-1, ALKAZAR). FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation may accelerate approval. Phase 3 trial actively enrolling. Expected completion March 2026.

safety_profile.rationale: Early data suggest favorable safety profile compared to lorlatinib. Designed to avoid CNS and metabolic toxicities. Less weight gain and hyperlipidemia reported. Full safety data maturing.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically designed for ALK+ NSCLC including compound ALK mutations. Active against broad spectrum of single and compound ALK resistance mutations.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Fourth-generation, brain-penetrant ALK inhibitor designed to potently inhibit wild-type ALK and a broad spectrum of ALK resistance mutations, including compound mutations (double mutations) that confer resistance to lorlatinib. Novel chemical scaffold distinct from earlier-generation ALK TKIs.
biomarker_requirements: Prior ALK TKI therapy (for current indication)
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial Only — FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation

treatments.name: Platinum/Pemetrexed Chemotherapy (Post-TKI Progression)

evidence_level.rationale: Multicenter retrospective studies with established efficacy data. ORR 29.7% in ALK+ NSCLC refractory to 2nd-gen ALK inhibitors. Standard chemotherapy backbone for non-squamous NSCLC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 4.3 months as monotherapy, extending to 6.8 months when combined with ALK TKI. ORR 29.7% with median DOR 6.4 months. Modest benefit but important salvage option after TKI exhaustion.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved, universally available worldwide. Standard of care chemotherapy regimen. No biomarker requirements beyond non-squamous histology.

safety_profile.rationale: Standard chemotherapy toxicities: myelosuppression, nausea, fatigue, nephrotoxicity (cisplatin). Pemetrexed relatively well-tolerated for chemotherapy. Requires B12/folate supplementation.

biomarker_match.rationale: ALK+ non-squamous NSCLC patients tend to have better response to pemetrexed-based chemotherapy than ALK-negative patients. No specific biomarker required.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Platinum agents (cisplatin/carboplatin) form DNA crosslinks causing cell death. Pemetrexed is a multi-targeted antifolate inhibiting thymidylate synthase, dihydrofolate reductase, and glycinamide ribonucleotide formyltransferase, disrupting DNA and RNA synthesis. Particularly effective in non-squamous histology.

treatments.name: Brigatinib After Alectinib/Ceritinib Failure (ALTA-2 Sequential)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 ALTA-2 trial (N=103) evaluated brigatinib in patients who progressed on alectinib or ceritinib. Confirmed ORR 26.2%, median PFS 3.8 months. Limited but prospectively collected data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Confirmed ORR of 26.2% with median PFS of 3.8 months. Limited activity in post-2nd-gen TKI setting. Inferior to lorlatinib in this sequential role. Some patients may achieve durable responses based on specific resistance mutation profiles.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for ALK+ NSCLC. Can be prescribed off-sequence. Available at all oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety profile from ALTA-1L. ILD risk requires monitoring but manageable with dose escalation strategy. Less metabolic toxicity than lorlatinib.

biomarker_match.rationale: Most effective against specific ALK resistance mutations (I1171N/T/S). Resistance mutation profiling via NGS recommended to predict response. Activity mutation-dependent.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Second-generation ALK TKI with potent activity against multiple ALK resistance mutations. Particularly effective against I1171N mutation that commonly arises after alectinib. Used sequentially when specific resistance mutation profiles favor brigatinib over lorlatinib.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — available for sequential use

treatments.name: Continuation ALK TKI + Platinum/Pemetrexed Combination (Beyond Progression)

evidence_level.rationale: Multicenter retrospective study (N=156) comparing PT/Pem + ALK TKI vs PT/Pem alone after first-line next-gen ALK TKI progression. Not prospectively validated but growing evidence supports this approach.

survival_benefit.rationale: PFS 6.6 vs 3.5 months (TKI+chemo vs chemo alone). OS 16.4 vs 11.4 months. Lower intracranial progression (18.7% vs 34.0% at 12 months). Meaningful improvement by continuing TKI with chemo.

accessibility.rationale: Uses FDA-approved drugs in combination. Can be prescribed at oncologist discretion. Growing acceptance in clinical practice though not yet in formal guidelines as preferred strategy.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ AEs in 28% with alectinib combo but 50% with lorlatinib combo. Additive toxicity is a concern, particularly lorlatinib metabolic effects combined with chemotherapy myelosuppression.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets both ALK-dependent and ALK-independent tumor cell populations. Rationale is strongest when mixed resistance mechanisms (on-target + off-target) are suspected.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines continued ALK TKI inhibition (targeting residual ALK-dependent clones) with cytotoxic chemotherapy (targeting ALK-independent resistant clones). Dual-pronged attack addresses tumor heterogeneity at progression. Continued TKI also maintains CNS disease control.

treatments.name: Gilteritinib — ALK/AXL Dual Inhibitor for Lorlatinib Resistance

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 clinical trial (NCT06225427) in ALK+ NSCLC post-lorlatinib. Preclinical/early clinical data show activity against lorlatinib-resistant compound mutations and AXL-mediated bypass resistance. Case reports showing >24 months disease control.

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical data demonstrate activity against ALK compound mutants and AXL bypass pathway. Case reports of successful long-term disease control (>24 months). Represents potential last-line targeted option after all ALK TKIs exhausted.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through clinical trial (NCT06225427). Gilteritinib is FDA-approved for FLT3+ AML, enabling potential off-label compassionate use, but not approved for NSCLC.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety from FLT3+ AML use. Main toxicities: transaminase elevation, myalgia/arthralgia, fatigue. Generally manageable side effect profile.

biomarker_match.rationale: Effective against ALK compound mutations (I1171N compounds) and AXL-mediated bypass resistance. Requires molecular profiling (NGS/liquid biopsy) to confirm resistance mechanism.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual FLT3/AXL inhibitor that overcomes lorlatinib resistance through two mechanisms: direct inhibition of ALK compound mutants (especially I1171N-containing compounds) and blockade of AXL-mediated bypass signaling pathway. AXL activation by macrophage-derived GAS6 is a key lorlatinib resistance mechanism.

key_evidence.journal: Nature Communications / Lung Cancer Targeted Therapy

biomarker_requirements: ALK compound mutations or AXL pathway activation
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial Only (NCT06225427) — potential off-label from AML approval

treatments.name: SHP2 Inhibitor + ALK TKI Combination (Bypass Resistance Strategy)

evidence_level.rationale: Preclinical data from gene screening studies identified SHP2 as a resistance node. SHP2 inhibitor SHP099 + ceritinib combination inhibited growth of resistant patient-derived tumor cells. Early-phase clinical exploration ongoing.

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical evidence of synergy in overcoming ALK TKI resistance. Targets off-target bypass resistance via RAS/MAPK pathway. Clinical benefit data not yet available.

accessibility.rationale: Preclinical to early clinical stage. SHP2 inhibitors (RMC-4630, TNO155, RLY-1971) in early trials for various indications. Not specifically available for ALK+ NSCLC outside research.

safety_profile.rationale: SHP2 inhibitors have shown manageable toxicity in early trials. Combination toxicity with ALK TKIs unknown. Potential for additive GI and hepatic toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically targets SHP2/RAS/MAPK bypass pathway. Requires molecular profiling to confirm bypass resistance mechanism. Most relevant for patients with off-target ALK TKI resistance.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: SHP2 (PTPN11) is a protein tyrosine phosphatase that acts as a signaling node upstream of RAS/MAPK pathway. In ALK TKI-resistant cells, SHP2 mediates bypass signaling enabling tumor survival despite ALK inhibition. Combining SHP2 inhibitors with ALK TKIs blocks both ALK and bypass pathways simultaneously.

clinical_trials.title: ALKOVE-1: A Study of NVL-655 (Neladalkib) in Patients with Advanced NSCLC and Other Solid Tumors Harboring ALK Rearrangement
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 31% in 253 TKI-pretreated patients; 46% in lorlatinib-naive; 86% in TKI-naive exploratory cohort. FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation granted. Durable responses with 80% lasting 12+ months in lorlatinib-naive cohort.

clinical_trials.title: ALKAZAR: Phase 3 Study of Neladalkib vs Alectinib in Treatment-Naive ALK-Positive NSCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase 3 randomized trial comparing 4th-gen neladalkib to alectinib as first-line treatment. Primary endpoint is PFS. No results yet — actively enrolling.

clinical_trials.title: FORGE-1: Phase 1/2 Study of TPX-0131 (Zotizalkib) in ALK+ Advanced/Metastatic NSCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: Fourth-generation ALK TKI targeting compound mutations. Trial terminated due to adverse risk/benefit profile assessment. Brain exposure limited by ABCB1 efflux. Preclinical potency against G1202R and L1196M mutations was promising but clinical translation insufficient.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 1 Study of Gilteritinib in Stage IV ALK-Positive NSCLC After Prior Treatment
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating FLT3/AXL inhibitor gilteritinib for lorlatinib-resistant ALK+ NSCLC. Preclinical data shows activity against compound mutations and AXL-mediated bypass resistance. Case reports of >24-month disease control in compassionate use.

clinical_trials.title: CROWN: Lorlatinib vs Crizotinib in Treatment-Naive ALK-Positive Advanced NSCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: 5-year data: median PFS not reached (lorlatinib) vs 9.3 mo (crizotinib). 5-year PFS rate 60% vs 8%. 92% reduction in intracranial progression. OS immature but trending favorably. Longest PFS ever reported for single-agent targeted therapy in any metastatic solid tumor.

clinical_trials.title: ALEX: Alectinib vs Crizotinib in Treatment-Naive ALK-Positive Advanced NSCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: Final OS: median OS 81.1 mo (alectinib) vs 54.2 mo (crizotinib), HR 0.67. First global RCT showing OS improvement for next-gen ALK TKI vs crizotinib. 5-year OS 62.5% vs 45.5%. Intracranial responses 64% vs 21%.

combination_strategies.rationale: Continuation of ALK TKI with chemotherapy at progression yields PFS 6.6 vs 3.5 months and OS 16.4 vs 11.4 months vs chemo alone. Lower intracranial progression (18.7% vs 34.0%). Addresses both ALK-dependent and independent resistant clones. More toxicity with lorlatinib combo (50% grade 3+) vs alectinib combo (28%).

SHP2 identified as a critical resistance node in ALK TKI-resistant cells via shRNA screening. SHP2 inhibitor + ceritinib combination inhibited growth of resistant patient-derived tumor cells. Targets RAS/MAPK bypass signaling that enables tumor survival despite ALK inhibition.

AXL-mediated resistance is enhanced by macrophage-derived GAS6 and MMP11+ fibroblasts in the tumor microenvironment. Gilteritinib as FLT3/AXL dual inhibitor can overcome lorlatinib resistance in ALK-rearranged cells. Case reports show >24-month disease control with gilteritinib in lorlatinib-resistant patients.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Sequential switch to neladalkib (NVL-655) at progression
combination_strategies.rationale: Neladalkib specifically designed to overcome compound ALK mutations (double mutations) that emerge after lorlatinib. ORR 31% in heavily pretreated patients. Phase 3 ALKAZAR trial will evaluate neladalkib head-to-head vs alectinib. Represents the future of sequential ALK TKI strategy from 3rd-gen to 4th-gen.

If bone metastases develop, denosumab significantly delays skeletal-related events vs zoledronic acid and improves median OS in NSCLC subgroup (9.5 vs 8.0 months). SRE rate drops from 33.8% to 8.8% during treatment. Should be initiated upon development of bone metastases.

supportive_care.evidence: Retrospective pharmacovigilance analysis (FAERS 2013-2024) and clinical management guidelines. 81% of lorlatinib patients require lipid-lowering agents. 22% need 2+ agents for cholesterol, 31% need 2+ for triglycerides.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents cardiovascular morbidity from treatment-induced hypercholesterolemia (82%) and hypertriglyceridemia (61%). Enables continued lorlatinib therapy without dose reduction. Statins and fibrates are first-line; pharmacologic mitigation preferred over dose modification.

supportive_care.approach: Weight Management Program (Addressing Obesity BMI 32 + TKI-Induced Weight Gain)
supportive_care.evidence: 2025 study: early weight gain >=5% on lorlatinib strongly associated with maximum weight gain. Patients <=50 years and those with grade 3 hypercholesterolemia at highest risk. Weight gain underreported in ALK+ NSCLC trials (18% gain >=10% on alectinib). Obesity medication options (semaglutide, liraglutide) under consideration.
supportive_care.benefit: Patient already has BMI 32 (obese), placing him at high risk for metabolic complications with ALK TKI therapy. Proactive weight management may mitigate cardiovascular risk, improve treatment tolerability, and maintain functional status. Interdisciplinary approach with nutritionist and endocrinologist recommended.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — given baseline obesity and young age (45)

supportive_care.evidence: COPD treatment independently associated with improved OS in patients with advanced NSCLC (multicenter study). Pulmonary rehabilitation improves FEV1, FVC, dyspnea severity, and exercise capacity equally in NSCLC patients with and without COPD. ILD/pneumonitis risk from ALK TKIs (2.14% all-grade incidence) requires monitoring.
supportive_care.benefit: Maximizing pulmonary function in a patient with mild COPD preserves performance status and treatment tolerance. Pre-treatment spirometry establishes baseline. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves QoL. COPD management ensures optimal candidacy for continued systemic therapy. Monitor closely for TKI-induced ILD (especially with brigatinib).

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN guidelines and expert consensus recommend routine brain MRI surveillance for ALK+ NSCLC patients given high propensity for CNS metastases. ALK is enriched in never-smokers and young patients. Early detection of subcentimeter asymptomatic brain metastases enables more treatment options.
supportive_care.benefit: ALK+ NSCLC has high CNS tropism. While lorlatinib provides 92% reduction in intracranial progression, surveillance remains important. If alectinib or brigatinib chosen instead, brain MRI every 3-6 months is essential. Early detection allows continued systemic TKI therapy without need for radiation in many cases.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — standard of care for ALK+ NSCLC

supportive_care.evidence: Clinical management guidelines for lorlatinib AEs. CNS effects: cognitive changes (28%, 2.9% severe), mood changes (21%, 1.7% severe), speech changes (11%), psychotic effects (7%), seizures (1.9%). Dose interruption with restart at reduced dose is recommended management.
supportive_care.benefit: Early recognition of neurocognitive changes enables prompt dose modification, preserving both treatment efficacy and quality of life. Multidisciplinary monitoring with neurology/psychiatry input recommended. Educate patient and caregivers on warning signs. Most CNS effects are mild and reversible with dose adjustment.

supportive_care.approach: Bone Health and Skeletal-Related Event Prevention
supportive_care.evidence: Phase 3 RCT comparing denosumab vs zoledronic acid in advanced cancers including NSCLC (N=1776, 44% NSCLC). Denosumab delayed SREs and improved OS in NSCLC subgroup (9.5 vs 8.0 months). Real-world data: SRE rate decreased from 33.8% to 8.8% with denosumab.
supportive_care.benefit: Bone metastases affect ~33% of advanced NSCLC patients. Proactive monitoring and initiation of bone-targeted agents upon bone metastasis development prevents pathologic fractures, spinal cord compression, and pain. Denosumab preferred over zoledronic acid in NSCLC based on OS advantage.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Moderate — initiate upon development of bone metastases
